title: Namespace
description: What a namespace is, why std::cout needs a namespace at all, using namespace vs using declarations, and creating your own namespace.
objectives: Understand why namespaces exist (avoiding name collisions)
Understand the difference between using namespace std; and a targeted using declaration
Read a basic custom namespace definition
Know that nested namespaces exist without needing them yet

sections.text: 1. What is a Namespace?
You've already typed using namespace std; at the top of every program so far. Today we look at what that line actually means.
A namespace is a named region that groups related names together, so the same name can exist in different namespaces without conflicting.
2. Why Do We Need Namespaces?

sections.title: Two files with the same name
sections.text: Imagine two files both named report.txt — one inside a Sales folder, another inside an HR folder. The name report.txt alone is ambiguous, but Sales/report.txt and HR/report.txt are not. A namespace works like that folder: it prefixes a name so it stays unambiguous even if the same short name is used elsewhere.
Without namespaces, if two different libraries both declared something named cout (or add, or List), the compiler wouldn't know which one you meant.
3. The std Namespace
The entire C++ Standard Library — cout, cin, vector, string, and everything else we'll learn — is grouped inside one namespace called std (short for "standard").

rows: The name we're accessing, from inside std

sections.text: 4. The Scope Resolution Operator ::
:: is used to say exactly which namespace (or scope) a name should be looked up in.
Read std::cout as: "cout, specifically the one that lives inside std."
5. using namespace std;
using namespace std; tells the compiler: for the rest of this scope, let me refer to every name inside std without writing std:: each time.

This is convenient for small learning programs, but in larger real-world projects it can pull in far more names than you need and occasionally cause naming clashes. Many teams avoid using namespace std; at the top of header files for this reason — an interview-worthy detail, even though we'll keep using it for simplicity while learning.
6. A More Targeted Option: using Declaration

rows: Brings in every name from std
Brings in only cout from std

sections.text: A using declaration for just the names you actually use is generally considered a more precise habit than importing an entire namespace.
7. Creating Your Own Namespace
We have not learned functions yet, so don't worry about the int add(int, int); syntax — only understand that a namespace can group declarations together.
This conceptually means: use the add function that lives inside the MyMath namespace.
8. Why Create Custom Namespaces?

In a large project, different parts of the code — or different libraries you depend on — might reasonably want to use the same short name, like add or List. Wrapping each part's code in its own namespace (MyMath, MyApp, ThirdPartyLib) keeps those names from colliding.

sections.title: Custom namespaces help with
points: Avoiding name collisions between your code and libraries
Grouping related declarations together, similar to how std groups the Standard Library
Making large codebases easier to navigate

sections.text: 9. Nested Namespaces (Just a Preview)
This is only a preview — namespaces can be nested inside each other, accessed as Company::Project::something. Not needed for our current learning stage.
10. Simple Example Without using namespace std;
To make the connection concrete, here's a program that uses std::cout directly, without importing the whole namespace:
Notice there's no using namespace std; line here — we simply write std::cout directly, and it works exactly the same way.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Thinking std is a keyword — std is just the name of a namespace, the Standard Library's namespace specifically.
Forgetting :: and writing something like std cout — the scope resolution operator :: is required between the namespace and the name.
Thinking using namespace std; is required — it's a convenience, not a requirement; std::cout works fine on its own.
Thinking namespaces are only for the Standard Library — any project can define its own namespaces to organize its own code.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a namespace in C++?
questions.answer: A namespace is a named region that groups related names together, preventing naming conflicts between different parts of a program or different libraries.

questions.question: What does the :: operator do?
questions.answer: The scope resolution operator :: specifies which namespace (or scope) a name should be looked up in, e.g. std::cout.

questions.question: What does using namespace std; do?
questions.answer: It brings every name from the std namespace into the current scope, so they can be used without the std:: prefix.

questions.question: Why might using namespace std; be discouraged in larger projects?
questions.answer: It imports every name from std at once, which can pull in more than needed and occasionally cause naming conflicts — especially risky inside header files that get included elsewhere.

questions.question: What is the difference between using namespace std; and using std::cout;?
questions.answer: using namespace std; imports all names from std, while using std::cout; imports only cout — a more targeted, precise choice.

questions.question: Can you create your own namespace?
questions.answer: Yes. Any project can define its own namespace to group related declarations and avoid name collisions with other code.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A namespace groups related names together so the same short name can be reused in different parts of a program without conflicting. The entire C++ Standard Library lives inside the std namespace, which is why we write std::cout, using the scope resolution operator ::. using namespace std; is a convenience that imports every std name at once, while a targeted using declaration like using std::cout; imports just what's needed. Projects can also define their own custom namespaces for the same reason.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Namespace
sections.text: Namespace = Folder for names. :: = "inside". std::cout = "cout, inside std." using namespace std; = "stop making me write std:: every time."
